rows.v: The first Vice President of the United States (1789–1797), serving under George Washington before becoming the second President.
Adams established the vice presidency's primary constitutional role as president of the Senate, casting a record number of tie-breaking votes.
Adams famously called the vice presidency 'the most insignificant office that ever the invention of man contrived,' setting an enduring tone of frustration with the role.
The second Vice President (1797–1801), serving under his political rival John Adams before winning the presidency himself.
Jefferson's service under an opposing-party president exposed a flaw in the original system, where the runner-up became vice president.
The awkward Adams–Jefferson pairing helped prompt the 12th Amendment (1804), which had presidential and vice-presidential candidates run on a single ticket.
The third Vice President (1801–1805) under Thomas Jefferson, and the most notorious figure ever to hold the office.
Burr killed former Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton in an 1804 duel while still serving as vice president, then was later tried for treason.
His tied electoral vote with Jefferson in 1800, thrown to the House of Representatives, directly spurred the 12th Amendment's reform of presidential elections.
The fourth Vice President (1805–1812), serving under both Jefferson and Madison. A longtime governor of New York.
Clinton was the first vice president to die in office, and the first to serve under two different presidents.
A leading Anti-Federalist, he had earlier opposed ratifying the Constitution, fearing it gave the federal government too much power over the states.
The fifth Vice President (1813–1814) under James Madison. A signer of the Declaration of Independence whose name became a lasting political term.
Gerry died in office in 1814, the second vice president to do so, leaving the office vacant for the rest of Madison's term.
As governor of Massachusetts, he approved a district so contorted it resembled a salamander, giving us the enduring word 'gerrymander.'
The seventh Vice President (1825–1832), serving under two presidents before resigning. One of the most formidable political minds of his era.
Calhoun was the first vice president to resign the office, stepping down to take a Senate seat and champion states' rights.
His fierce advocacy of nullification and slavery made him a towering and divisive figure whose ideas foreshadowed the coming sectional crisis.
The eighth Vice President (1833–1837) under Andrew Jackson, and the first to be elected president directly from the office in modern fashion.
Van Buren was a master political organizer, credited with helping build the modern Democratic Party and the spoils system.
Born after the Declaration of Independence, he was the first president and vice president who was a citizen of the United States rather than a British subject.
The ninth Vice President (1837–1841) under Martin Van Buren, and the only vice president ever chosen by the Senate.
When no candidate won an electoral majority in 1836, the Senate elected Johnson under the 12th Amendment's contingency procedure.
He was popularly, if dubiously, credited with killing the Shawnee leader Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames, a claim central to his political fame.
The 15th Vice President (1861–1865), Abraham Lincoln's first-term running mate, dropped from the ticket for Lincoln's 1864 reelection.
Hamlin's replacement by Andrew Johnson placed Johnson, not the antislavery Hamlin, in the presidency after Lincoln's assassination.
A staunch opponent of slavery, Hamlin is one of history's great might-have-beens, narrowly missing the presidency that Reconstruction would soon define.
The 16th Vice President (1865), who became president just weeks into the term after Lincoln's assassination.
Johnson is the only person to serve as vice president, then president, and later be impeached, surviving his Senate trial by a single vote.
A Southern Democrat kept on Lincoln's ticket for unity, his clashes with Congress over Reconstruction made his presidency one of the most contentious in history.
The 17th Vice President (1869–1873) under Ulysses S. Grant. A former Speaker of the House caught in a major scandal.
Colfax is one of only two people to serve as both Speaker of the House and Vice President.
His career collapsed in the Crédit Mobilier railroad bribery scandal, and he was dropped from the ticket for Grant's second term.
The 22nd Vice President (1889–1893) under Benjamin Harrison. A wealthy banker and diplomat.
Morton had earlier declined the vice-presidential nomination alongside James Garfield, who was assassinated; the man who accepted instead became president.
His decision to refuse the 1880 nomination is a striking historical near-miss, as that very office led directly to the White House.
The 24th Vice President (1897–1899) under William McKinley. An influential adviser who died in office.
Hobart restored the vice presidency's prestige as a close confidant of McKinley, then died in office in 1899.
His death opened the 1900 vice-presidential slot for Theodore Roosevelt, setting in motion Roosevelt's rise to the presidency the very next year.
The 25th Vice President (1901) under William McKinley, who became president after just six months when McKinley was assassinated.
At 42, Roosevelt became the youngest person ever to assume the presidency, transforming the office with his energetic, activist style.
Party bosses had pushed him into the 'powerless' vice presidency to sideline him, a plan that backfired spectacularly when McKinley was shot.
The 26th Vice President (1905–1909) under Theodore Roosevelt. A conservative senator from Indiana.
Fairbanks represented the Republican Party's conservative wing, an ideological counterweight to the progressive Roosevelt.
The city of Fairbanks, Alaska, was named in his honor, a lasting mark from an otherwise quiet tenure in the office.
The 28th Vice President (1913–1921) under Woodrow Wilson, serving two full terms during World War I.
When Wilson was incapacitated by a stroke in 1919, Marshall declined to assume presidential powers, as no clear procedure then existed.
That constitutional gray area, with the nation effectively leaderless, helped inspire the 25th Amendment's presidential disability provisions decades later.
The 30th Vice President (1925–1929) under Calvin Coolidge. A banker and diplomat with a Nobel Peace Prize.
Dawes won the Nobel Peace Prize for the Dawes Plan, which restructured Germany's punishing World War I reparations.
He is also the only vice president to have co-written a number-one pop song; his melody later became the standard 'It's All in the Game.'
The 32nd Vice President (1933–1941) under Franklin D. Roosevelt, serving two terms before a bitter falling-out.
Garner broke with FDR over the court-packing plan and a third term, a rare open rift between a president and vice president.
He is famous for reportedly calling the vice presidency not worth 'a bucket of warm spit,' a blunt verdict on the office's limited power.
The 33rd Vice President (1941–1945) under Franklin D. Roosevelt during World War II, dropped from the 1944 ticket.
Wallace's replacement by Harry Truman proved pivotal, as FDR died months into the term, making Truman president instead.
A progressive idealist, Wallace's ouster at the 1944 convention is one of history's great turning points, shaping the early Cold War's course.
The 34th Vice President (1945) under Franklin D. Roosevelt, who became president after just 82 days when FDR died.
Truman was kept so uninformed as vice president that he learned of the atomic bomb only after becoming president.
His abrupt elevation amid World War II underscored the stakes of the vice presidency, thrusting an unprepared man into history's gravest decisions.
The 35th Vice President (1949–1953) under Harry Truman. A beloved Senate veteran who coined the office's nickname.
Barkley's grandson suggested the nickname 'Veep' for the office, a term that has endured ever since.
At 71 the oldest man to take the office at the time, his folksy charm helped rehabilitate the vice presidency's public image.
The 36th Vice President (1953–1961) under Dwight Eisenhower, who later became president himself in 1969.
Nixon expanded the vice presidency's visibility, taking on diplomatic missions and a far more public role than predecessors.
His 'Checkers speech,' saving his spot on the 1952 ticket amid a funds scandal, was a pioneering use of television in American politics.
The 37th Vice President (1961–1963) under John F. Kennedy, who became president after Kennedy's assassination.
Johnson took the oath aboard Air Force One hours after Kennedy was shot, then drove landmark civil rights legislation through Congress.
A legendary Senate majority leader before the office, his masterful command of legislative power defined his consequential presidency.
The 38th Vice President (1965–1969) under Lyndon Johnson, and the Democratic nominee who narrowly lost the 1968 presidential race.
Humphrey championed civil rights for decades, having electrified the 1948 convention with a landmark speech on the issue.
His loyalty to Johnson's Vietnam policy as vice president hampered his own 1968 campaign, a cautionary tale of the office's constraints.
The 40th Vice President (1973–1974), the first appointed under the 25th Amendment, who then became the only unelected president.
Ford was appointed after Spiro Agnew resigned, then became president when Nixon resigned, never having been elected to either office.
His unprecedented path to the White House, entirely through appointment and resignation, was a unique test of the 25th Amendment's machinery.
The 42nd Vice President (1977–1981) under Jimmy Carter, who transformed the office into a genuine governing partnership.
Mondale is credited with redefining the modern vice presidency, securing a West Wing office and a true advisory role for the first time.
His partnership model with Carter became the template every subsequent vice president has followed, turning a ceremonial post into real influence.
The 43rd Vice President (1981–1989) under Ronald Reagan, who then won the presidency in 1988.
Bush was the first sitting vice president since Martin Van Buren in 1836 to be elected directly to the presidency.
His broad résumé as the office's holder, from the CIA to the UN, exemplified the modern vice presidency as a launchpad to the top job.
The 45th Vice President (1993–2001) under Bill Clinton, and the central figure in one of the closest elections in history.
Gore won the national popular vote in 2000 but lost the presidency after a Supreme Court ruling halted the Florida recount.
He later won the Nobel Peace Prize and an Academy Award for his climate-change advocacy, a remarkable post-office second act.
The 46th Vice President (2001–2009) under George W. Bush, widely regarded as among the most powerful ever to hold the office.
Cheney wielded unprecedented influence over national security and foreign policy, especially after the September 11 attacks.
His expansive vision of vice-presidential and executive power made him a defining, and deeply debated, figure of the post-9/11 era.
The 47th Vice President (2009–2017) under Barack Obama, who later won the presidency himself in 2020.
Biden drew on decades of Senate experience to serve as a key legislative negotiator and adviser throughout Obama's two terms.
His path from the office to the presidency four years later echoed a route walked by Nixon, marking him among the most experienced figures in modern politics.
The 49th Vice President (2021–2025) under Joe Biden, the first woman and first person of South Asian and Black heritage in the office.
Harris broke a historic barrier as the first female vice president, and as a senator had cast tie-breaking votes of unusual frequency.
A former senator and California attorney general, her election marked a milestone in the long expansion of who could hold national executive office.
